toxicity_notes: Ripe fruit not toxic and widely eaten; foliage, stems, and green (unripe) fruit contain solanine and are toxic if ingested in quantity.
Cooked tubers are a staple food and safe; green or sprouted tubers and all green above-ground parts (leaves, stems, fruit) contain solanine and are toxic if ingested.
Fruit is edible when cooked; foliage and unripe/raw plant parts contain solanine and are toxic if ingested in quantity.

Generally considered toxic: unripe (green) berries and foliage contain solanine and glycoalkaloids. Fully ripe black berries of some regional varieties are eaten in small quantities in parts of Africa/Asia after cooking, but toxicity varies by variety and ripeness -- regional practice is inconsistent, so treat as toxic unless a locally verified edible variety and full ripeness are confirmed.
Ripe fruit pulp is edible and cultivated as a food crop (skin is bitter, usually discarded); leaves, stems, and unripe fruit contain solanine and can cause gastrointestinal upset if ingested.

soil_type: well-drained loam, rich in organic matter
toxicity_notes: Ripe fruit is a cultivated food crop and edible; foliage and unripe fruit contain solanine, a toxic compound typical of the genus, causing nausea/vomiting/diarrhea if ingested.

soil_type: well-drained loam, rich in organic matter
toxicity_notes: Ripe fruit is a cultivated food crop and considered non-toxic to humans and pets; as with related Solanum species, foliage is generally assumed to carry some solanine risk, but specific documentation for this species is limited -- not well documented for foliage.
Contains glycoalkaloids (solasodine, solamine, solasodiene) and other bioactive alkaloids typical of Solanum; considered toxic if ingested, consistent with genus-wide solanine-type toxicity.
Edible and non-toxic; capsaicin in some cultivars causes irritation/burning sensation on contact or ingestion, not true toxicity.
Edible and non-toxic; very high capsaicin content causes strong irritation/burning on contact or ingestion, not true toxicity.
Edible and non-toxic; capsaicin causes irritation on contact or ingestion, not true toxicity.
Edible and non-toxic; capsaicin causes irritation on contact or ingestion, not true toxicity.
Ripe fruit is edible and cultivated as a food crop; unripe fruit and foliage contain solanine-like glycoalkaloids and are toxic if ingested.
Ripe fruit (once husk dries/splits) is edible and a culinary staple; unripe green fruit still in tight green husk and foliage contain solanine-like compounds and are toxic if ingested.
Highly toxic; contains nicotine throughout the plant (leaves especially), which can be fatal in concentrated or ingested/absorbed form (e.g., green tobacco sickness from skin contact).
Not well documented; generally regarded as having low toxicity and not commonly listed as a significant poisoning risk to humans or pets, but comprehensive toxicological data are lacking.
Highly toxic in all parts; contains tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine) and can be fatal if ingested.
Highly toxic in all parts; contains tropane alkaloids, hallucinogenic and dangerous, with risk of severe poisoning or death if ingested.

common_names: Night-blooming jasmine|Night jessamine|Lady of the night
toxicity_notes: Toxic berries and leaves; can cause severe illness with fatalities reported, especially in children and pets.
Ripe berries are a widely consumed food crop and edible; leaves and unripe fruit carry some minor cautions but are generally considered safe as food.

Foliage and stems are reported toxic to mammals (typical Solanaceae alkaloids), though toxicity is debated and parts have been eaten/used medicinally after processing in some regions; unlikely to be dangerous except in large quantities.
Toxic in all parts, especially unripe berries; contains solanine and related glycoalkaloids, can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea if ingested.

Root used medicinally in Ayurveda; generally regarded as safe at typical doses but has cautions (e.g., pregnancy, thyroid conditions, potential liver effects) -- not a straightforward toxicity case, best described as used medicinally with some contraindications.

Not well documented for humans/pets; one variety (var. violacea) has been implicated in field neurotoxicity outbreaks in calves in South Africa and is suspected toxic to horses and poultry, so livestock caution is warranted.

Not well documented for humans/pets specifically; the whole plant is reportedly pounded and used as a fish poison in traditional use, and most Solanaceae contain alkaloids, suggesting some toxic potential, but formal toxicology data are lacking.
